name: Cross-Compile for Multiple OS/Arch Targets
description: Build Go binaries for Linux, macOS, and Windows across amd64 and arm64 architectures from a single machine.

steps.title: Build for a specific target
steps.description: Set GOOS and GOARCH environment variables to cross-compile for a different platform.
steps.tip: Run 'go tool dist list' to see all supported OS/arch combinations.

steps.title: Build for macOS ARM (Apple Silicon)
steps.description: Compile a binary targeting macOS on Apple Silicon processors.

steps.title: Build for Windows
steps.description: Produce a Windows executable with the .exe extension.

steps.title: Build all targets with a script
steps.description: Automate multi-platform builds by looping over target combinations.
steps.command: for pair in linux/amd64 linux/arm64 darwin/amd64 darwin/arm64 windows/amd64; do GOOS=${pair%/*} GOARCH=${pair#*/} go build -o bin/myapp-${pair%/*}-${pair#*/} ./cmd/myapp; done

steps.title: Verify binary targets with file command
steps.description: Confirm that each binary was compiled for the correct platform.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Go Cross-Compile for Multiple Targets
set -euo pipefail

APP=${1:?"Usage: $0 <app-name>"}
CMD_PATH=${2:-"./cmd/$APP"}
OUTPUT_DIR="bin"

TARGETS=(
  "linux/amd64"
  "linux/arm64"
  "darwin/amd64"
  "darwin/arm64"
  "windows/amd64"
)

mkdir -p "$OUTPUT_DIR"

echo "=== Cross-Compiling $APP ==="
for target in "${TARGETS[@]}"; do
  GOOS=${target%/*}
  GOARCH=${target#*/}
  OUTPUT="$OUTPUT_DIR/$APP-$GOOS-$GOARCH"
  [ "$GOOS" = "windows" ] && OUTPUT="$OUTPUT.exe"

  echo "Building $GOOS/$GOARCH -> $OUTPUT"
  GOOS=$GOOS GOARCH=$GOARCH go build -ldflags="-s -w" -o "$OUTPUT" "$CMD_PATH"
done

echo ""
echo "=== Build Artifacts ==="
ls -lh "$OUTPUT_DIR"/
file "$OUTPUT_DIR"/*

faq.question: Do cross-compiled binaries need CGO?
faq.answer: By default Go disables CGO for cross-compilation (CGO_ENABLED=0). If your code uses CGO, you need a cross-compiler toolchain like zig cc or musl-cross.

faq.question: How do I embed version info into the binary?
faq.answer: Use ldflags: go build -ldflags "-X main.version=1.0.0 -X main.commit=$(git rev-parse HEAD)" to inject variables at build time.
